Consider the sets \( T_n = \{n, n + 1, n + 2, n + 3, n + 4\} \), where \( n = 1, 2, 3,\dots, 96. \) How many of these sets contain \( 6 \) or any integral multiple thereof (i.e., any one of the numbers \( 6, 12, 18, \dots)? \)

Consider the sequence where the \( n^{\text{th}} \) term \( t_n = \frac{n}{n+2}, n=1, 2, \dots. \) The value of \( t_3 \times t_4 \times t_5 \times \dots \times t_{53} \) equals:



Quantitative Aptitude: CAT 2006 | Question: 55

Directions for the below question:

Let \( S \) be the set of all pairs \( (i, j) \) where \( 1 \leq i \leq j < n \) and \( n \geq 4 \). Any two distinct number of \( S \) are called ‘friends’ if they have one constituent of the pairs in common and ‘enemies’ otherwise. For example, if \( n=4 \), then \( S=\{ (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (2, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4) \} \). Here, \( (1, 2) \) and \( (1, 3) \) are friends, \( (1, 2), (2, 3) \) are also friends, but \( (1, 4) \) and \( (2, 3) \) are enemies.

For general, \( n \) how many enemies will each member of \( S \) have?



Quantitative Aptitude: CAT 2007 | Question: 04

Directions for the below question:

Let \( S \) be the set of all pairs \( (i, j) \) where \( 1 \leq i \leq j < n \) and \( n \geq 4 \). Any two distinct number of \( S \) are called ‘friends’ if they have one constituent of the pairs in common and ‘enemies’ otherwise. For example, if \( n=4 \), then \( S=\{ (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (2, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4) \} \). Here, \( (1, 2) \) and \( (1, 3) \) are friends, \( (1, 2), (2, 3) \) are also friends, but \( (1, 4) \) and \( (2, 3) \) are enemies.

For general \( n \), consider any two members of \( S \) that are friends. How many other members of \( S \) will be common friends of both these members?

2)r+1

3)r

4) The new series is not in G.P.

here G.P Is geometric progression
